title: Why Transitions Between Activities Are So Activating
researcherTopic: Internal Family Systems, parts work, Self-energy
metaDescription: Discover why simple transitions between activities can feel so activating for those with complex trauma (C-PTSD). Learn how your nervous system's wiring and past experiences contribute to this common survival response.
imageDescription: A warm, luminous image of golden light filtering through a window, illuminating the delicate, intricate web of a spider plant. The focus is on the interplay of light and shadow, suggesting the complex and beautiful inner workings of the nervous system. The overall feeling is one of peace, resilience, and gentle growth.
htmlContent:
What happens when the body remembers something the mind has spent decades trying to forget? For those of us who have navigated the complex territory of developmental trauma, the answer often lies in the surprisingly fraught moments of transition—the simple, seemingly innocuous shifts from one state of being to another. It’s the space between finishing a work email and starting to cook dinner, the pause after a phone call ends, or the brief, quiet moments after a friend leaves. For a nervous system wired for survival, these gaps are not empty; they are potent, charged with the echoes of past experiences, and they can become profoundly activating in ways that defy simple explanation. We are not reacting to the present moment’s transition, but to the accumulated weight of a thousand unsafe transitions that came before.

The Interruption of Flow as a Threat Signal

For a nervous system that has found a precarious sense of equilibrium, any change can feel like a threat. We might find ourselves in a state of relative calm or focus—a state we’ve worked hard to achieve—and the interruption of that state, the requirement to shift our attention and energy, can feel like a profound violation. It’s not the new activity itself that is the problem, but the breaking of the fragile peace we had established. This isn’t a conscious choice, but a deeply ingrained, somatic response. The body, having learned that the world is an unpredictable and often dangerous place, interprets the interruption as a sign that it must once again be on guard. It is a return to a state of hypervigilance, a state that is exhausting and unsustainable, yet feels like the only way to survive. We might find ourselves resisting the transition, procrastinating, or feeling a surge of anger or anxiety that seems disproportionate to the situation. This is the body’s wisdom, distorted by trauma, trying to protect us from a danger that is no longer present but feels imminently real. We are not lazy or undisciplined; we are simply trying to manage a nervous system that is doing exactly what it was designed to do in a world that no longer exists.

The wellness industry sells solutions to problems it helps you believe you have.

The Body's Grammar of Unsafety

Complex trauma teaches the body a language of unsafety, a grammar of activation that it never forgets. Every cell holds the memory of what it felt like to be unsafe, and transitions can act as a powerful trigger for these somatic memories. It’s not a cognitive process; it’s a felt sense. The shift from a state of rest to a state of activity, or vice versa, can be enough to bring the body back to a time when such shifts were perilous. Perhaps it was the sound of a parent’s footsteps approaching, the sudden silence that preceded an outburst, or the chaotic energy of a household in turmoil. The body remembers the feeling of dread, the tightening in the chest, the shallowing of the breath. And so, in the present moment, as we move from one activity to another, the body can react as if the original trauma is happening all over again. This is where the work of researchers like Dick Schwartz becomes so illuminating. His model of Internal Family Systems (IFS) helps us understand that we are not a single, monolithic self, but a collection of ‘parts,’ each with its own history, beliefs, and burdens. When we experience a difficult transition, it is often because one of our parts—a young, wounded part—has been activated. This part doesn’t know that we are now an adult, that we are safe, that the danger has passed. It only knows the terror of the past, and it is trying to protect us in the only way it knows how. By learning to listen to these parts, to understand their stories, and to offer them the compassion and care they never received, we can begin to heal the deep wounds of the past.

The body has a grammar. Most of us never learned to read it.

The Cognitive Cost of Shifting Gears

The challenges of transitions are not just somatic; they are also cognitive. Complex trauma can have a profound impact on executive functioning—the set of mental skills that include working memory, flexible thinking, and self-control. These are the very skills that are required to navigate transitions smoothly. When we are asked to shift from one task to another, we are asking our brains to disengage from one set of neural pathways and engage another. For a brain that is already struggling with the cognitive load of managing trauma, this can be an overwhelming task. It’s like asking a computer that is already running at 99% capacity to open a new, resource-intensive program. The system slows down, it freezes, it might even crash. This is why we might find ourselves staring blankly at the next task on our to-do list, unable to muster the mental energy to begin. It’s why we might feel a sense of mental fog or confusion when we are in the midst of a transition. It’s not a sign of intellectual deficiency, but a testament to the incredible amount of energy our brains are expending just to keep us functioning. We are not just managing the demands of the present moment; we are also managing the echoes of the past, the constant, low-level hum of the trauma that is always there, just beneath the surface. For more on the cognitive impacts of trauma, you might find our article on understanding the traumatized brain to be a helpful resource.

The Echo of Unmet Needs

Our difficulties with transitions in the present are often a direct echo of our experiences in the past. For a child to develop a healthy, regulated nervous system, they need a sense of predictability and safety. They need to know that their needs will be met, that they will be cared for, that they are loved. When these fundamental needs are not met, when a child grows up in an environment of chaos, neglect, or abuse, they learn that the world is an unsafe place. They learn that they cannot rely on others to meet their needs, that they must be constantly on guard, that they must be prepared for the worst. Transitions, in this context, become fraught with danger. The end of a caregiver’s presence could mean being left alone for hours on end. The start of a new activity could mean being subjected to criticism or abuse. The child learns to associate transitions with a sense of dread and foreboding. As adults, we carry these early experiences with us. We may not consciously remember them, but our bodies do. And so, when we are faced with a transition, our bodies can react with the same fear and anxiety that we felt as children. We are not just reacting to the present moment; we are reacting to a lifetime of unmet needs. This is why healing from complex trauma is not just about changing our thoughts or behaviors; it’s about tending to the wounded child within, about providing the safety, care, and love that was so painfully absent in the past. A related article on the long shadow of childhood neglect explores this in more detail.

Awareness doesn't need to be cultivated. It needs to be uncovered.

The Nervous System's Predictive Power

The nervous system is a prediction machine. It is constantly, and largely unconsciously, scanning the environment and our internal state to anticipate what will happen next. This predictive capacity is a cornerstone of our survival. When the nervous system can accurately predict safety, it can downshift into a state of rest and social engagement. When it predicts danger, it mobilizes for fight or flight. For those who have experienced complex trauma, this predictive function becomes skewed. The system, having been trained in an environment of chronic threat, learns to over-predict danger. It sees threat where there is none, and it interprets ambiguous signals as signs of impending harm. Transitions are, by their very nature, ambiguous. They are a moment of uncertainty, a brief period of not-knowing. For a nervous system that is already biased towards threat, this uncertainty is intolerable. It is a breeding ground for catastrophic predictions. The mind may not be consciously aware of these predictions, but the body responds as if they are already happening. The heart rate increases, the muscles tense, the breath becomes shallow. We are, in effect, living in a future that has not yet happened, a future that is colored by the unresolved pain of the past. A client once described this to me as feeling like she was constantly bracing for an impact that never came. This is the lived experience of a nervous system that has lost its capacity to accurately predict safety. The work of healing, then, is not to stop the nervous system from making predictions, but to gradually, and gently, retrain it to make new ones. It is to provide it with enough experiences of safety in the present that it can begin to release its grip on the past. It is to show it, through embodied experience, that a transition can be just a transition, not a prelude to disaster. For those wanting to explore this topic in greater depth, our article on rewiring the hypervigilant nervous system offers a comprehensive guide.

The Space Between: A Territory of Embodied Awareness

While the challenges of transitions are real and profound, they also offer us a powerful opportunity for healing. The space between activities, the pause, the gap—this is the territory of embodied awareness. It is a place where we can begin to notice, without judgment, what is happening in our bodies and minds. It is a place where we can begin to cultivate a different relationship with our experience, one that is based on curiosity and compassion rather than fear and resistance. Instead of trying to push through the discomfort of a transition, what if we were to simply pause and notice it? What if we were to bring a gentle, curious attention to the sensations in our bodies, to the thoughts in our minds, to the emotions that are arising? This is not about fixing or changing our experience, but about simply being with it. It is about learning to tolerate the discomfort, to stay present with the activation, to ride the waves of our experience without being swept away by them. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve sat with people who describe this as a profound shift in their relationship with themselves. It is a move from a state of constant struggle to a state of gentle, compassionate presence. It is a move from a life lived in reaction to a life lived in response. And it is in this space, this territory of embodied awareness, that the real work of healing begins. For those interested in exploring this further, our article on the practice of somatic mindfulness offers a deeper dive.

The algorithm of your attention determines the landscape of your experience.

Cultivating a New Relationship with Transitions

Healing from the activating nature of transitions is not about eliminating the activation itself, but about changing our relationship to it. It is about learning to meet the activation with a sense of curiosity and compassion, rather than with fear and resistance. It is about understanding that the activation is not a sign that we are broken, but a sign that our nervous system is trying to protect us. And it is about learning to provide our nervous system with the safety and support it needs to finally, after all these years, stand down. This is not a quick or easy process. It requires patience, practice, and a great deal of self-compassion. But it is possible. It is possible to learn to navigate the spaces between with a sense of ease and grace. It is possible to transform these moments of activation into moments of connection and healing. And it is possible to come to a place where transitions are no longer a source of dread, but an opportunity for a deeper, more intimate relationship with ourselves. This is the promise of the healing journey—not a life without pain, but a life in which we are no longer defined by it.

The research is clear on this, and it contradicts almost everything popular culture teaches.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so exhausted after a simple transition?

This is a common experience for those with C-PTSD. The exhaustion you feel is not just mental, but physical. Your nervous system is expending a tremendous amount of energy to manage the perceived threat of the transition. It is a state of high alert, and it is incredibly draining. Be kind to yourself in these moments. Rest when you need to. And know that the exhaustion is a sign of how hard your body is working to keep you safe.

How can I support myself during a difficult transition?

One of the most powerful things you can do is to simply pause. Before you move from one activity to another, take a few moments to check in with yourself. Notice the sensations in your body, the thoughts in your mind, the emotions that are present. Take a few deep breaths. And then, when you feel ready, move into the next activity with a sense of gentle, compassionate awareness. This simple act of pausing can make a world of difference.

Will I ever be able to handle transitions without getting activated?

Healing is not about getting rid of the activation, but about changing your relationship to it. Over time, as you continue to do the work of healing, you will likely find that the activation becomes less intense and less frequent. You will develop a greater capacity to stay present with the activation without being overwhelmed by it. And you will come to a place where you can navigate transitions with a sense of greater ease and confidence. But the goal is not to eliminate the activation, but to meet it with wisdom and compassion.

What if I can't feel anything in my body during transitions?

Numbness and dissociation are common responses to trauma. If you can’t feel anything in your body, that’s okay. Don’t try to force it. Simply notice the numbness. Bring a gentle, curious attention to it. Over time, as your nervous system begins to feel safer, you may find that sensation begins to return. But for now, simply being with the numbness is enough. It is a courageous act of presence.

In our work at Kalesh.love, we explore the path of healing through the lens of embodied consciousness, offering a way to come home to yourself. For those seeking further information from a clinical perspective, the National Institute of Mental Health and the National Center for Biotechnology Information provide a wealth of research and resources on the impacts of trauma.

What we are ultimately seeking is not a life without difficulty, but a life in which we have the capacity to meet our difficulties with an open and compassionate heart. It is a journey of returning to ourselves, of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have lost, of coming home to the truth of who we are. And in the tender space of our own hearts, we find that we have everything we need to heal.
